issue_title: Emitting: blank line between tables
issue_body:
```python
print(toml.dumps({'foo':{'a':1}, 'bar': {'b': 2}}))
```

Gives:

```toml
[foo]
a = 1
[bar]
b = 2
```

That's valid, but I think it's more readable with a blank line between tables (i.e. just before `[bar]`). The [examples](https://github.com/toml-lang/toml#user-content-example) are generally written with blank lines between tables.

issue_title: execute_every with a period of 0 causes infinite loop
issue_body:
Looking at process_timeout, the code looks like this:

```
            while self.delayed_commands:
                command = self.delayed_commands[0]
                if not command.due():
                    break
                command.function(*command.arguments)
                if isinstance(command, PeriodicCommand):
                    self._schedule_command(command.next())
                del self.delayed_commands[0]
```

If one of the delayed_commands is a PeriodicCommand with delay of 0 (as created by execute_every with period of 0), the _schedule_command will always re-add the command immediately after running it, and it will satisfy the command.due() on the subsequent iteration, causing an infinite loop.

At the very least, the IRC client should raise an error if one attempts to create such a command. Even better would be to support periodic commands with a delay of 0 (which one would want to run every timeout, but only once per).

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/12
- Originally reported by: Jason R. Coombs
- Originally created at: 2013-01-01T15:04:51.722

issue_title: Start supporting pyvenv
issue_body:
Supporting pyvenv (the stdlib venv module) might mean that in a remote future, where Python <3.4 will have disappeared, the virtualenv dependency will be redundant.

Due to [this bug](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/python3.4/+bug/1290847), to use venv on Ubuntu/Debian it'd require a preemptive check if `ensurepip` is available with `python -c 'import ensurepip'` (`python -m ensurepip` won't work due to a debian patch)

Still, before implementing it, we'd wait until some [bugs](https://bugs.python.org/issue24875) will be ironed out

issue_title: zdesk should use the requests library / multipart file uploads are near impossible
issue_body:
It's very hard to handle multipart file uploads, which some endpoints require for their POST payloads, however the requests module can handle this kind of encoding easily. There is probably no downside to switching from httplib2 to requests, although it would be a breaking change for anyone using the `complete_response` parameter, as we would start sending back requests responses rather than httplib2 responses.

Using requests would allow zdesk to better handle, and perhaps automatically handle, any complex encoding requirements.

    def requests_call(self, method, url, headers={}, params=None, data=None, stream=False):

issue_title: receive_timeout no longer applies when Connection.start_tls is called
issue_body:
Took me quite a while to debug this. I'm on a 64 bit Ubuntu 16.04 install, using Python 3.5. I'm testing by establishing a VPN connection, opening an LDAP connection, disconnecting the VPN after some use, and then attempting to still interact with the same connection object.

If you set the `receive_timeout` parameter on a Connection object, it will no longer have an effect once you invoke `start_tls` - any interactions via the socket do not seem to timeout appropriately.

I believed this was because of the way the Python ssl library appears to work - wrapping a socket involves dismantling the original socket object and transporting attributes to a new SSLSocket instance (and presumably, any socket options set were not propagated across).

However, I've just tried to manually set the socket option to an SSLSocket object to restore the timeout (this is done after `start_tls` is invoked):
```
conn.socket.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_RCVTIMEO, pack('LL', 10, 0))
```

But doing this hasn't fixed the issue - if I then try to use the connection, it still hangs rather than timing out after 10 seconds.

I'm not sure whether this is an issue with how Python implements SSL, or an issue with ldap3, or whether this is just something that is incompatible with SSL and needs documenting.

issue_title: Main example is not Py2/3 safe
issue_body:
Split out from #11 

Looking at the [example](https://github.com/cocagne/txdbus#usage-example) on the frontpage, I'm a bit puzzled that none of the arguments there is using explicit `b'..'` or `u'...'`. So the arguments will be bytes on Py2 and unicode on Py3. Are the arguments auto-coerced under the hood?

Take

```python
    notifier = yield con.getRemoteObject('org.freedesktop.Notifications',
                                         '/org/freedesktop/Notifications')
```

Does `getRemoteObject` take bytes or strings? If the latter, then the example code should read:

```python
    notifier = yield con.getRemoteObject(u'org.freedesktop.Notifications',
                                         u'/org/freedesktop/Notifications')
```

This code then works on both Python 2 and 3.

issue_title: Column names differ from VMD.  Were they chosen intentionally?
issue_body:
I just started using biopandas and it seems nice.  Toward promoting discussion related to its design, I have a general question regarding the column names, particularly for `df['ATOM']`.  Note that I work with simulations of molecular structures so I care most about the `df['ATOM']` field.

Coming from using VMD for several years, I noticed many differences to access the PDB fields.  I have not compared to Chimera or PyMol, but below is a comparison between biopandas, VMD, and the [documentation for the PDB file format](http://www.wwpdb.org/documentation/file-format-content/format33/sect9.html#ATOM).  I would prefer the VMD options, partially because they are familiar but they are also more succinct.  They are probably not as clear as a verbose name, but they are a closer match to the PDB field.

@rasbt, did you have strong motivation for the selections you made?  If anyone else have other ideas or preferences, I would be interested to hear them.

```
BIOPANDAS        VMD        | COLUMNS        DATA  TYPE    FIELD        DEFINITION
----------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
record_name      atom       |  1 -  6        Record name   "ATOM  "
atom_number      index      |  7 - 11        Integer       serial       Atom  serial number.
atom_name        name       | 13 - 16        Atom          name         Atom name.
alt_loc          altLoc     | 17             Character     altLoc       Alternate location indicator.
residue_name     resname    | 18 - 20        Residue name  resName      Residue name.
chain_id         chain      | 22             Character     chainID      Chain identifier.
residue_number   resid      | 23 - 26        Integer       resSeq       Residue sequence number.
insertion        insertion  | 27             AChar         iCode        Code for insertion of residues.
x_coord          x          | 31 - 38        Real(8.3)     x            Orthogonal coordinates for X in Angstroms.
y_coord          y          | 39 - 46        Real(8.3)     y            Orthogonal coordinates for Y in Angstroms.
z_coord          z          | 47 - 54        Real(8.3)     z            Orthogonal coordinates for Z in Angstroms.
occupancy        occupancy  | 55 - 60        Real(6.2)     occupancy    Occupancy.
b_factor         beta       | 61 - 66        Real(6.2)     tempFactor   Temperature  factor.
segment_id       segname    |
element_symbol   element    | 77 - 78        LString(2)    element      Element symbol, right-justified.
charge           charge     | 79 - 80        LString(2)    charge       Charge  on the atom.
```

issue_title: Namespace packages are not supported
issue_body:
Goto is tricked by namespace packages and fails.

I made tests to demonstrate this issue in https://github.com/flupke/jedi/commit/ad95279f1f5d160a22d5e07c1ced2a3011f39777

The problem is that `imp.find_module()` does not support namespace packages.

Should `imports.ImportPath._follow_file_system()` fiddle with `pkg_resources` (see http://stackoverflow.com/questions/5741362/alternatives-to-imp-find-module for a beginning of solution)? Or is `imp.find_module()` something you would like to discard in the future? (maybe by using `importlib.import_module()`, which does work with namespace packages, but has the disadvantage of actually importing the modules)

issue_title: [XEP-0084] Allow access to the original id (sha-1 of the image) to prevent issues with case sensitivity
CRITICAL error ConnectionLost when crochet does not cancel the deferred
issue_body:
I encountered an issue with crochet not terminating the twisted deferred correctly when raising the TimeoutError exception. 

### Code for reference
```python
def test_eventual_result_timeout(server_url):

    # server incredibly slow
    eventual_result = fido.fetch(server_url + '100sec')

    # make sure no timeout error is thrown here but only on result retrieval
    assert eventual_result.original_failure() is None

    with pytest.raises(crochet.TimeoutError):
        eventual_result.wait(timeout=1.0)

    assert eventual_result.original_failure() is None
    print("Sleeping a bit to make sure a potential exception in the reactor"

    "thread due to connectionLost is trapped in eventual_result")
    time.sleep(5)

    assert eventual_result.original_failure() is None

    print("Finished sleeping. in a moment we will lose references to"
    "eventual_result and hence the deferred will be closed uncorrectly causing"
    "the CRITICAL message to be logged right after this message....")


@crochet.run_in_reactor
def fetch_inner(url, method, headers, body, timeout, connect_timeout):

    bodyProducer, headers = _build_body_producer(body, headers)

    deferred = Agent(reactor, connect_timeout).request(
        method=method,
        uri=url,
        headers=listify_headers(headers),
        bodyProducer=bodyProducer)

    def response_callback(response):
        finished = Deferred()
        response.deliverBody(HTTPBodyFetcher(response, finished))
        return finished

    deferred.addCallback(response_callback)

    return deferred


def fetch(
    url, method='GET', headers=None, body='', timeout=DEFAULT_TIMEOUT,
    connect_timeout=DEFAULT_CONNECT_TIMEOUT,
    content_type=DEFAULT_CONTENT_TYPE, user_agent=DEFAULT_USER_AGENT,
):

    crochet.setup()
    return fetch_inner(url, method, headers, body, timeout, connect_timeout)
```

**Traceback**
Everything was going fine (TimeoutError is correctly raised) but the twisted reactor thread was failing due to the connection closing incorrectly. Traceback:
```
Sleeping a bit to make sure a potential exception in the reactor thread due to 
connectionLost is trapped in eventual_result

Finished sleeping. in a moment we will lose references to eventual_result and hence
 the deferred will be closed uncorrectly causing the CRITICAL message to
 be logged right after this message....

CRITICAL:twisted:Unhandled error in EventualResult
Traceback (most recent call last):
Failure: twisted.web._newclient.ResponseNeverReceived: [<twisted.python.failure.Failure twisted.internet.error.ConnectionLost: Connection to the other side was lost in a non-clean fashion: Connection lost.>]
```

All assertion were true, showing that no exception is trapped in the EventualResult. Exception happens exactly when the eventual_result is garbage collected.

### The problem
From [Twisted documentation](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/15.4.0/core/howto/defer.html#unhandled-errors):
> If a Deferred is garbage-collected with an unhandled error (i.e. it would call the next errback if there was one), then Twisted will write the error’s traceback to the log file. This means that you can typically get away with not adding errbacks and still get errors logged. Be careful though; if you keep a reference to the Deferred around, preventing it from being garbage-collected, then you may never see the error (and your callbacks will mysteriously seem to have never been called).

In this case the ConnectionLost exception is caused by the garbage collector disposing of the deferred when we lose all references to the EventualResult (and hence the deferred). In fact we don't see anything logged until the eventual_result is alive.

I think that raising TimeoutError [here](https://github.com/itamarst/crochet/blob/master/crochet/_eventloop.py#L193) without disposing of the deferred is causing the Agent connection to be closed incorrectly and the exception to be thrown in the reactor thread **after** raising the TimeoutError.

**Verification**
As a last step to verify the problem I added self.cancel() before raising the TimeoutError so that the deferred is cancelled properly. 
```python
 if not self._result_set.is_set():
     self.cancel()
     raise TimeoutError()
``` 
I tested this with our internal acceptance test suite.

**Why not handling twisted.internet.error.ConnectionLost with a twisted errback?**
The problem is a systematic erroneous disposal of the deferred object causing the connection to be always incorrectly closed the same way. Let's say I was to handle this on our side this way:
```python
    def handle_timeout_errors(error):
        if error.check(twisted.web.client.ResponseNeverReceived):
            if error.value.reasons[0].check(
                twisted.internet.error.ConnectionLost
            ):
                # we get here if crochet aborts the connection uncorrectly
                # when the EventualResult timeout is due. Nothing to raise
                # because crochet will take care of raising a TimeoutError.
                return
        return error

    deferred.addErrback(handle_timeout_errors)
```
 I would be mistakenly catching all potential connectionLost issues, even the ones due to other causes (for example the server aborting the connection).

### Action points
I understand crochet would not want to dispose of deferred objects before raising the TimeoutError to allow multiple retries on result retrieval.

**Q:** Would crochet be interested in a way to dispose/destroy the deferred ? Something as [this on stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/a/865272). Otherwise I am conducting the same discussion to handle this [on our side](https://github.com/Yelp/fido/issues/31).

issue_title: Escape sequences are unprocessed on Windows with TERM set
issue_body:
On Windows, when the environment variable `TERM` is set to any value, escape sequences will be printed unprocessed (no colored output). Concerning the values of `TERM`/the terminals where this happens, I'm still waiting for user feedback (see msiemens/PyGitUp#24).

According to `git bisect` this behaviour has been introduced in 961428e6c1c3a6855da5dedccfc418f295b54c25.

issue_title: [rewrite] Member not found using DM mention to bot

issue_title: kafka.KafkaConsumer can become "stuck" on a down broker
issue_body:
While stress testing Kafka I've noticed that kafka.KafkaConsumer can become "stuck" on a down broker.  If the broker fails after bootstrapping the consumer does not reconnect to one of the other brokers, but instead goes into a loop reporting connect errors.  When disconnected the following message is repeatedly thrown:

```
Connect attempt to <BrokerConnection host=<broker hostname> port=9092> returned error 111. Disconnecting.

A critical component here is that the broker hostname in the error is determined via bootstrapping through the Kafka server, it isn't a member of the user provided bootstrap list.

issue_title: Imphash implementation
issue_body:
Hi,

Having a database to resolve ordinal imports is nice but if you consider this to calculate imphash, other software should use the same database in order to match imphashes correctly. Another problem is the "ord" string concatenated with the ordinal number. Other software would have to respect that in order to get the same hash result.

That's tha way pefile is currently working with imphash if I understood it correctly:

Given a file import data ready to be send for MD5 calculation to get imphash result:

`ws2_32.getsockname,kernel32.rtlmovememory,kernel32.getmailslotinfo,msvbvm60.ord585,msvbvm60.ord586`

The file imported function 8 of WS2_32.DLL and functions 585 and 586 from MSVBVM60.DLL. Thanks to ordlookup database, the 8th function of WS2_32.DLL was resolved to getsockame(). In the other hand, 
Function 585 of  MSVBVM60.DLL is not the database, so the name "ord" + 585 is used. The same for function 586.

In the name of interoperability I suggest to change imphash behaviour to ignore ordlookup database and do not add this "ord" string on ordinal functions, so the result would be:

`ws2_32.6,kernel32.rtlmovememory,kernel32.getmailslotinfo,msvbvm60.585,msvbvm60.586`

Function names cannot start with numbers, so we don't need to be afraid of conflicts. ;)

I know some systems are using imphash already and this change would probably impact them but actually if more modules are added to ordlookup, currently imphash will break anyway, so I think you should consider fix this problem now. If you agree, I'll be glad to send the patch.

Thanks for your attention!

issue_title: Tablesorter sometimes not being loaded into jQuery with require()
issue_body:
On 2.30.3, we've occasionally had a very odd issue, where Tablesorter is loaded, but not added to jQuery.fn, so that we end up with "tablesorter is not a function" errors.

```coffee
$ = require('jquery')
tablesorter = require('tablesorter')

settings = {
  theme: 'materialize'
}

$(document).on 'load', () ->
  window.tablesorter = tablesorter

  $("table.sortable").each (index, element) ->
      $(element).tablesorter(settings) # sometimes fails with a "$(...).tablesorter is not a function"
```

It seems like it's a weird dependency/build order thing, because sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't, but in every case, `window.tablesorter` is set. We haven't found a way to solve this issue permanently, except by changing the way we initialise tablesorter, to basically duplicate the existing $.fn = in tablesorter (yuck!):

```coffee
  $("table.sortable").each (index, element) ->
    # merge & extend config options
    c = $.extend( true, {}, tablesorter.defaults, settings, tablesorter.instanceMethods )
    # save initial settings
    c.originalSettings = settings
    # create a table from data (build table widget)
    tablesorter.setup element, c
```

I'm wondering if it's possible/sensible to add a method to Tablesorter that allows it to be setup on an arbitrary element, without needing to go though $/jQuery? What do you think?

For example:

```coffee
$ = require('jquery')
tablesorter = require('tablesorter')

$(document).on 'load', () ->
  $("table.sortable").each (index, element) ->
    tablesorter.apply(element)  # or tablesorter.apply($, element)
```

issue_title: Multi-project Analysis
issue_body:
Sometimes it may be useful to organise packages into projects and analyse a whole project at a time.

Index files kind of solve this issue, by defining an index file for each project, but the analysis results overlap. Haros always uses the same analysis database file. Adding an option to load a different file could also solve this, at the expense of making the command-line a little more cumbersome.

In any case, the visualiser is not adapted for this concept, so multiple visualisers would have to be exported, one for each project. This requires launching multiple viz servers (when hosting), or creating a top-level `index.html` with links to open the individual projects, which is not ideal.

My proposal is to add a new `Project` data structure.

```python
class Project(object):
    def __init__(self, name, packages = None):
        self.name = name
        self.packages = packages if not packages is None else []
```

Index files could define a `name` for a project, or default to the file name minus extension.
The `AnalysisManager` could store the history for each project separately, or simply create one analysis database for each project. The exported *viz* files and directory structure would have to change. Instead of `data/packages.json`, etc., it would have to go one level deeper, `data/project/packages.json`. Viz itself would have to be changed as well.

issue_title: Management of BBMD specific requests in simple (and foreign) application server.
issue_body:
For non BBMD device, the annex J of standard specification indicates the server should reply with BVLC result specifying error code.
Concerned services are:
* Write-Broadcast-Distribution-Table
* Read-Broadcast-Distribution-Table
* Register-Foreign-Device
* Read-Foreign-Device-Table
* Delete-Foreign-Device-Table-Entry
* Distribute-Broadcast-To-Network

These services are well managed in `BIPBBMD` class from `bvllservice.py` but not in `BIPSimple` and `BIPForeign`. I added in `confirmation`, the treatment for each of these pdu which sends a static result (with corresponding code). 
See attached patch for implementation details.

[0016_BBMDRequestMngt.txt](https://github.com/JoelBender/bacpypes/files/1707984/0016_BBMDRequestMngt.txt)

issue_title: Verify the parameters can be serialized
issue_body:
`Log.note("{{data}}", data=d)` places no limit on `d`:  The parameter is packed and not inspected until it is time to serialize it.  At serialization time the serialization happens and we run out of stack space, or run out o memory crashing the program.

Add serialization limits so we do not run out of memory.  Ensure the logging library reports back to original call that passed the `d`.

issue_title: Boolean() validation woes
issue_body:
```
>>> from voluptuous import *
>>> schema = Schema({ Required('a'): Boolean() })
>>> schema({ 'a': [{'foo':'bar'}]})
{'a': True}
```

Is this expected behavior?  This is the shortened code snippet:
```python
>>> def Boolean(v):
...     if isinstance(v, basestring):
...         v = v.lower()
...         if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
...             return True
...         if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
...             return False
...         raise ValueError
...     return bool(v)
```
It seems like it will only return `True` and `False` for the indicated values, and then return `True` for any populated value that is not of type `str` or `unicode`:

Dict:
```
>>> schema({ 'a': {'foo':'bar'}})
{'a': True}
```

Int:
```
>>> schema({ 'a': 99})
{'a': True}
```
Float:
```
>>> schema({ 'a': 123.45})
{'a': True}
```

It doesn't seem like a good idea to validate `Boolean()` if I can pass a list to the schema check and have it accidentally validate as True.

This can be partially solved by using `All(Any(str, unicode), Boolean())`, but this is not what I would _expect_ to need to do with the validator.  The problem I have is that after importing YAML data, `exclude: False` becomes `{'exclude': False}`, rather than `{'exclude': 'False'}`, which leads me to the current ugly, final solution:

```
Any(bool, All(Any(str, unicode), Boolean()))
```

There is nothing in your documentation that informs me of these hoops I have to jump through.  If I didn't read the code, I would have had no idea that it expects a string value.

As far as potential fixes go:
```
>>> def Boolean(v):
...     if isinstance(v, basestring):
...         v = v.lower()
...         if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
...             return True
...         if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
...             return False
...         raise ValueError
...     elif isinstance(v, bool):
...         return v
...     else:
...         raise ValueError
...
>>> Boolean(1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 12, in Boolean
ValueError
>>> Boolean("1")
True
>>> Boolean("0")
False
>>> Boolean(True)
True
>>> Boolean(False)
False
>>> Boolean({'a':'b'})
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 12, in Boolean
ValueError
```
It seems pretty straightforward to add the `elif` to test if it's a `bool` before attempting to return `bool(v)`, or more appropriately, `v`, since it's an instance of `bool`.

It could also be modified to work with floats and ints (not just strings of `"1"`, and `"0"`:
```python
def Boolean(v):
    # Make floats into integers
    if isinstance(v, float):
        # Ensure that only 0.0 is 0
        v = 1.0 if v > 0 and v < 1 else v
        v = int(v)
    # Make integers strings for guaranteed comparisons 
    if isinstance(v, int):
        v = str(v)
    if isinstance(v, basestring):
        v = v.lower()
        if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
            return True
        if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
            return False
        raise ValueError
    elif isinstance(v, bool):
        return v
    else:
        raise ValueError
```

I'm happy to submit this as a PR if you're satisfied this won't break existing, expected behavior.

issue_title: GPG signatures for source validation
issue_body:
As we all know, today more than ever before, it is crucial to be able to trust our computing environments. One of the main difficulties that package maintainers of GNU/Linux distributions face, is the difficulty to verify the authenticity and the integrity of the source code. With GPG signatures it is possible for packagers to verify source code releases quickly and easily.

#### Overview of the required tasks:
* Create and/or use a **[4096-bit RSA keypair][1]** for the file signing
* Use a **[strong, unique, secret passphrase][2]** for the key
* Upload the public key to a **[key server][3]** and **[publish the full fingerprint][4]**
* **[Sign][5]** every new Git **[commit][6]** and **[tag][7]**
* Create **[signed][8], [compressed][9]** (xz --best) release **archives**
* Upload a **[strong message digest][10]** (sha512) of the archive
* Configure **[HTTPS][11]** for your download server

### GPGit
[GPGit][12] is meant to bring GPG to the masses. It is not only a Python script that automates the process of [creating new signed Git releases with GPG][13], but also a [quick-start-guide][14] for learning how to use GPG. GPGit integrates perfectly with the [Github Release API][15] for uploading.

The security status of GNU/Linux projects will be tracked in the [Linux Security Database][16]. If you have any further questions, do not hesitate to [contact me][17] personally. Thanks for your help in making GNU/Linux projects more secure by using GPG signatures.

Thanks in advance.

[1]: https://github.com/NicoHood/gpgit#12-key-generation

issue_title: Using setup.cfg not .isort.cfg
issue_body:
isort allows using an `[isort]` section in `setup.cfg` rather than using a separate `.isort.cfg` file. (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/isort says "Or, if you prefer, you can add an isort section to your project’s setup.cfg with any desired settings.")

The benefit of doing this is that then the configuration is passed to the [git hook](https://bitbucket.org/tarek/flake8/src/a209fb69350c572c9b2d7b4b09c7657be153be5e/flake8/hooks.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default#hooks.py-18), but it isn't if the configuration is just in `.isort.cfg`.

The git hook presents another problem as flake8 doesn't allow the passing of options to it, so we can't pass the option to not check for it.

One solution to this would be to check for the file and the section in `setup.cfg` and only error if neither were found - ie, to extend `search_isort_config`. This would have the extra benefit that if you weren't using `.isort.cfg`, you wouldn't have to pass the option every time you ran `flake8`.

I'll see if I can get a pull-request together, but please let me know if there's something I've missed.

issue_title: Unterminated string exception in object containing empty string
issue_body:
# Setup

JSON data containing an empty string, e.g.:

 ```python
import json_tricks

json_data = u'{"foo": "", "bar": "http://google.com"}'
data = json_tricks.loads(json_data)
```

# Expected behaviour

Data is parsed successfully.

# Observed behaviour

The following exception occurs:

```python
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-50-b6ad4747ea59> in <module>()
      2
      3 json_data = u'{"foo": "", "bar": "http://google.com"}'
----> 4 data = json_tricks.loads(json_data)

C:\Users\chris\Miniconda2-x86\envs\db-dev-copy\lib\site-packages\json_tricks\nonp.pyc in loads(string, preserve_order, ignore_comments, decompression, obj_pairs_hooks, extra_obj_pairs_hooks, cls_lookup_map, allow_duplicates, conv_str_byte, **jsonkwargs)
    199         hooks = tuple(extra_obj_pairs_hooks) + obj_pairs_hooks
    200         hook = TricksPairHook(ordered=preserve_order, obj_pairs_hooks=hooks, allow_duplicates=allow_duplicates)
--> 201         return json_loads(string, object_pairs_hook=hook, **jsonkwargs)
    202
    203

C:\Users\chris\Miniconda2-x86\envs\db-dev-copy\lib\json\__init__.pyc in loads(s, encoding, cls, object_hook, parse_float, parse_int, parse_constant, object_pairs_hook, **kw)
    350     if parse_constant is not None:
    351         kw['parse_constant'] = parse_constant
--> 352     return cls(encoding=encoding, **kw).decode(s)

C:\Users\chris\Miniconda2-x86\envs\db-dev-copy\lib\json\decoder.pyc in decode(self, s, _w)
    362
    363         """
--> 364         obj, end = self.raw_decode(s, idx=_w(s, 0).end())
    365         end = _w(s, end).end()
    366         if end != len(s):

C:\Users\chris\Miniconda2-x86\envs\db-dev-copy\lib\json\decoder.pyc in raw_decode(self, s, idx)
    378         """
    379         try:
--> 380             obj, end = self.scan_once(s, idx)
    381         except StopIteration:
    382             raise ValueError("No JSON object could be decoded")

ValueError: Unterminated string starting at: line 1 column 20 (char 19)
```

**Note that this works as expected using `json.loads(...)`.**

# Workaround

If the data is split onto separate lines, the issue seems to go away, e.g.:

```python
import json
import json_tricks

json_data = u'{"foo": "", "bar": "http://google.com"}'
# Reformat JSON data onto separate lines.
json_data = json.dumps(json.loads(json_data), indent=4)
print(json_data)
# The following works now.
json_tricks.loads(json_data)
```

issue_title: Error when raising server errors in Python 3.
issue_body:
In Python 3, when the server sends back an error and the client tries to raise an exception, this happens:

    >>> from deluge_client import DelugeRPCClient
    >>> c = DelugeRPCClient('10.0.3.3', 58846, 'wrong_username', 'wrong_password')
    >>> c.connect()
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/home/mostafa/source/deluge-client/deluge_client/client.py", line 60, in connect
        result = self.call('daemon.login', self.username, self.password)
      File "/home/mostafa/source/deluge-client/deluge_client/client.py", line 102, in call
        exception = type(exception_type, (Exception, ), {})
    TypeError: type() argument 1 must be str, not bytes

This is due to the fact that `rencode.loads` function returns a `bytes` object in Python 3 not an `str` while the `type` function expects a string.

issue_title: Feature Request: generalize Doc.get_metadata
issue_body:
Basically it is very similar to what the current YAML filter is doing, except it is not for CodeBlock but for the actual YAML used in the Markdown metadata.

I see that you have an example manipulating the metadata in [panflute-filters/unformat_abstract.py at master · sergiocorreia/panflute-filters](https://github.com/sergiocorreia/panflute-filters/blob/master/filters/unformat_abstract.py). But in cases if we don't need to manipulate the metadata, but just to extract some information from there, getting the metadata in a dictionary is more natural. And similar to the current YAML filter, rather than reinventing the wheel each time in a filter, may be it will be more useful to add it in panflute as part of the "batteries".

On the other hand, I understand that such a filter is quite different from the current YAML filter, since the original YAML metadata is lost from pandoc.

issue_title: Certificates not valid on Chrome when running standalone proxy
issue_body:
This does not affect selenium tests and is only an issue when running Selenium Wire in standalone mode with:

`python -m seleniumwire standaloneproxy port=12345`

When using Chrome with the standalone proxy it reports that the server's certificate is not valid. This is because the certificate is created without a Subject Alternative Name. This is not a problem on older versions of Chrome, but is on newer versions as Chrome has tightened up its security.

The solution is to ensure that OpenSSL sets the Subject Alternative Name when generating certificates. There is a command line option for this in newer versions of OpenSSL but not in older versions.

issue_title: signing with CKM_RSA_PKCS_PSS fails with "Segmentation fault"
issue_body:
#### Your system information

* PyKCS11 version: nCipher PKCS#11 2.19.1, cryptokiVersion: 2.1
* Python version: 2.7.6

#### Please describe your issue in as much detail as possible:
I was trying to sign some data using a RSA-4096 key and **CKM_RSA_PKCS_PSS** as mechanism but calling of sign() function aborts with a "Segmentation fault":

```
data = "a932d16a6b0a383b873aa73e7bbe56631924775abd69e8ec5265d5719a4df37d9300f3217aaf078139b4fbe403c3800e" # SHA-384 of a file
signature = session.sign(privKey, binascii.unhexlify(data), PyKCS11.Mechanism(PyKCS11.CKM_RSA_PKCS_PSS)) 
```

The behavior is the same for all CKM_RSA_PKCS_PSS mechanisms (e.g. also for CKM_SHA384_RSA_PKCS_PSS) although they get listed for the selected slot by getMechanismList(). 

Using a "regular" CKM_RSA_PKCS mechanism returns the signature as expected -- it only fails when PSS padding is involved.

According to the API it should be possible to provide specific parameters for a mechanism but I'm not sure how to properly make the call:

`mechanism = PyKCS11.Mechanism(PyKCS11.CKM_RSA_PKCS_PSS, param=(PyKCS11.CKM_SHA384, PyKCS11.CKG_MGF1_SHA384, 48))`

...fails with "ValueError: byte must be in range(0, 256)" (as CKM_SHA384 equals 608). 
I noticed that LowLevel.py defines a class "CK_RSA_PKCS_OAEP_PARAMS" (with attributes "hashAlg", "mgf" and others) but I couldn't find a class for "CK_RSA_PKCS_PSS_PARAMS" (with expected attributes "hashAlg", "mgf" and "sLen".

**Could it be that support for PSS is missing or is it me who's using it wrong?**

#### Steps for reproducing this issue:

1. get list of available slots: getSlotList()
2. read slot info to find a slot with CKF_TOKEN_PRESENT: getSlotInfo(slot)
3. open appropriate session: session = pkcs11.openSession(slot, PyKCS11.CKF_SERIAL_SESSION)
4. find CKO_PRIVATE_KEY object: privKey = session.findObjects(...)[0]
5. call signing function as described above

Best Regards
Lars

issue_title: Angle Brackets Bug
issue_body:
I noticed that in some scenarios, this bug can occur: Suppose your string is the following: ``` >>> string = """ ... Start of test. ... ... for (i=0; i<3; i++) { ... printf("Hello, world."); ... } ... ... > This is a quote ... ... End of test. ... """ ``` I would expect the C-code snippet to be formatted as a `code` block, and the quote to be formatted as a `blockquote`. However, when I use `safe_mode="escape"` (because I want users of this to be able to use HTML in the application), `markdown2` thinks that there is a big HTML tag in this block from the less than symbol to the beginning of the blockquote: ``` >>> import markdown2 >>> markdown2.markdown(string, safe_mode="escape") '

Start of test.

\n\n
for (i=0; i<3; i++) {\n        printf("Hello, world.");\n    }  \n\n> This is a quote\n
\n\n

End of test.

\n' >>> markdown2.markdown(string, safe_mode="replace") '

Start of test.

\n\n
for (i=0; i[HTML_REMOVED] This is a quote\n
\n\n

End of test.

\n' >>> ``` This seems to be a bug, because it means I cannot have a `blockquote` after a block of code that uses a `<` symbol.

issue_body:
We seem to have lost support for this during the 2.0.0 refactor that included bravado-core as a dependency.

See https://github.com/striglia/pyramid_swagger/blob/master/tox.ini#L2 for where we disabled testing.

```

To complete this ticket, we need to:

issue_title: Set-Cookie header is injected into static content requests, breaking caching.
issue_body:
Right now, if `LoginManager()` is installed in a flask-app, it adds a `set-cookie` header to ALL requests, *including* static content.

This breaks (at least) Nginx reverse-proxy caching, as [nginx disables caching](http://stackoverflow.com/a/9232739/268006) if it sees a set-cookie header.

Realistically, setting cookies on static content is kind of silly anyways, and probably shouldn't happen in the first place.

It'd be nice if flask only set cookies on things that actually can change.

issue_title: BetaGeoFitter.conditional_probability_alive returns 0D-array, not float
issue_body:
The docstring mentions that the return type of `BetaGeoFitter.conditional_probability_alive` is `float`, but when passing values from DataFrames, the result is actually a 0-dimensional `ndarray`:

```
In [101]: bgf = BetaGeoFitter()

In [102]: df = load_cdnow_summary()

In [103]: bgf.fit(df['frequency'],df['recency'],df['T'])
Out[103]: <lifetimes.BetaGeoFitter: fitted with 2357 subjects, a: 0.79, alpha: 4.41, b: 2.43, r: 0.24>

In [104]: bgf.conditional_probability_alive(df.iloc[0]['frequency'],df.iloc[0]['recency'
     ...: ],df.iloc[0]['T'])
Out[104]: array(0.7266095443377226)

In [105]: 
```

The same method in other fitters seem to all have forms that would indeed return `float`s, but I haven't tested that explicitly.

I can make a PR where either the docstring is altered, or the return value is wrapped with `float()`; any thoughts on which would be preferable?

issue_title: Python 3.7 Nightly failures due to Python bug or travis
issue_body:
[See for example this job.](https://travis-ci.org/pytest-dev/pytest/jobs/313008163) [They fail because of a bug in python introduced for 3.7.](https://bugs.python.org/issue31845)

The state of this bug is 'fixed' on the bugtracker. So I think there is two possible problems:
- travis "nightly" is not actually the head of the master branch of cpython
- this bug has reappeared
I tried to find out the answer to the former, but could not ascertain. The log says it is running ``3.7.0a0`` which is not [the latest ``3.7.0a3``](https://docs.python.org/3.7/whatsnew/3.7.html).

issue_title: jsondiff 1.20 includes extraneous 'value' field for remove operation

$ cat has-friend.json
{ "name": "fred", "friend": "barney", "spouse": "betty" }
$ cat no-friend.json
{ "name": "fred", "spouse": "betty" }

$ jsondiff --version
jsondiff 1.20
$ jsondiff has-friend.json no-friend.json
[{"path": "/friend", "value": "barney", "op": "remove"}]

$ /usr/local/bin/jsondiff --version
jsondiff 1.16
$ /usr/local/bin/jsondiff has-friend.json no-friend.json
[{"path": "/friend", "op": "remove"}]

My reading of https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6902#section-4.2 is the 1.16 version is correct.

issue_title: add some way to handle request failures when using inlines
issue_body:
Hello @rolando ,

 I'm using inline_requests in my project and I just stumbled on unusual behavior regarding request errors. Basically it seems like when using inline_requests errors from middleware (e.g. retry middleware. offsite middleware) are not handled properly. By "not handled properly" I mean: they are silently ignored in some cases and they abort callback function.

This will be more clear with some example:

```python
class DemoSpider(Spider):
    name = "foo.com_discovery"
    start_urls = ["http://httpbin.org"]

    @inline_requests
    def parse(self, response):
        url = "http://httpbin.org/status/{}"
        for i in [400, 200]:
            response = yield Request(url.format(i))
            # this line will never be executed, crawl will stop after failed retries of 400
            if response.status == 200:
                # this will never get printed
                print("success" * 99)
```

This produces following behavior:

```
2016-04-25 13:03:12 [scrapy] DEBUG: Crawled (200) <GET http://httpbin.org> (referer: None, latency: 0.30 s)
2016-04-25 13:03:13 [aa.middlewares.retry] DEBUG: Retrying <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (failed 1 times): 400 Bad Request
2016-04-25 13:03:13 [aa.middlewares.retry] DEBUG: Retrying <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (failed 2 times): 400 Bad Request
2016-04-25 13:03:14 [aa.middlewares.retry] WARNING: Gave up retrying <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (failed 3 times): 400 Bad Request
2016-04-25 13:03:14 [scrapy] DEBUG: Crawled (400) <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (referer: http://httpbin.org, latency: 0.14 s)
2016-04-25 13:03:14 [scrapy] DEBUG: Ignoring response <400 http://httpbin.org/status/400>: HTTP status code is not handled or not allowed
2016-04-25 13:03:14 [foo.com_discovery] DEBUG: Scheduled 0 new requests
```

As you see second request to resource returning 200 status is not scheduled at all. After receiving 400 inline_requests are not going to process anything else and spider will close.

Expected behavior would be:

```
2016-04-25 13:12:20 [scrapy] DEBUG: Crawled (200) <GET http://httpbin.org> (referer: None, latency: 0.30 s)
2016-04-25 13:12:21 [scrapy] DEBUG: Crawled (200) <GET http://httpbin.org/status/200> (referer: http://httpbin.org, latency: 0.14 s)

2016-04-25 13:12:21 [aa.middlewares.retry] DEBUG: Retrying <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (failed 1 times): 400 Bad Request
2016-04-25 13:12:22 [aa.middlewares.retry] DEBUG: Retrying <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (failed 2 times): 400 Bad Request
2016-04-25 13:12:22 [aa.middlewares.retry] WARNING: Gave up retrying <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (failed 3 times): 400 Bad Request
2016-04-25 13:12:22 [scrapy] DEBUG: Crawled (400) <GET http://httpbin.org/status/400> (referer: http://httpbin.org, latency: 0.14 s)
2016-04-25 13:12:22 [scrapy] DEBUG: Ignoring response <400 http://httpbin.org/status/400>: HTTP status code is not handled or not allowed
2016-04-25 13:12:22 [foo.com_discovery] DEBUG: Scheduled 0 new requests
```

so expected behavior is: dont abort processing on middleware exception but continue on.

Do you think this is a bug worth fixing? Is this some known issue? I could try to figure out some way to fix it and create PR if we agree this would be useful addition.

issue_title: Empty strings not rejected when using a ModelSerializer
issue_body:
Hi,

I have come across a minor bug when using `django-phonenumber-field` with Django Rest Framework: when creating a `ModelSerializer` for a model that has a required `PhoneNumberField`, the serializer does not reject empty strings.

How to reproduce:

```python
from django.db import models
from phonenumber_field.modelfields import PhoneNumberField
from rest_framework import serializers


class MyModel(models.Model):
    # A mandatory phone number
    phone_number = PhoneNumberField()
    
    
class MySerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = MyModel
        fields = (
            'phone_number',
        )

MySerializer(data={'phone_number': ''}).is_valid()  # True when it should be False
```

From my understanding, the reason is that Django Rest Framework uses a [`ModelField`](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/fields/#modelfield) when it encounters unknown model fields, such a field does not treat the empty string as an empty value whereas `phonenumber_field.phonenumber.to_python` does. So the `ModelField` does not reject `''` and `validate_international_phonenumber` does not either.

A workaround is to use the provided PhoneNumberField serializer field, ex:

```python
from phonenumber_field.serializerfields import PhoneNumberField


class MySerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    phone_number = PhoneNumberField()

    class Meta:
        model = MyModel
        fields = (
            'phone_number',
        )
```

But as everything else works out of the box, people might not realize
that they need to.

issue_title: Table redefine with lazy_table
issue_body:
The following:
```
from pydal import DAL, Field
db = DAL("sqlite:memory", lazy_tables=True,migrate=False)
db.define_table('t_a', Field('code'))
print 'code' in db.t_a
db.define_table('t_a', Field('code_a'), redefine=True)
print 'code' in db.t_a
print 'code_a' in db.t_a
```
print
```
True
True
False
```
while with lazy_tables=False
```
True
False
True
```

issue_title: how to retrieve attachments from document
Add dbcopy to a view?
Remove src/trg optional parameters in replicator API
issue_body: Building a replicator with `source` and `target` parameters does not work and has a bug (referenced in #337) that's existed since 2.0.0.  Since having both `source/target` & `source_db/target_db` params can be confusing and `source/target` currently don't work, we will remove these optional params.

issue_title: Add support for Cloudant Search
issue_body:
We need to add support for creating Search Indexes in a DesignDocument as well as support for performing query searches through the `_search` endpoint targeting a Search Index.

This issue is dependent upon the completion of the bug fix #103.

issue_title: Fix CouchDatabase.create_document to handle DesignDocuments appropriately
issue_body:
- Currently `database.CouchDatabase.create_document()` creates a Document regardless of whether the document is a `Document` or a `DesignDocument`.  

- We need to add logic to ensure that if the "_id" field passed in as part of the data starts with '_design/' then this method should create a `DesignDocument` object and that DesignDocument object should be attached to the locally cached database dictionary.

issue_title: C++ batchnorm CPU version training well, but validation wrong
issue_body:
For bugs or installation issues, please provide the following information.
The more information you provide, the more likely people will be able to help you.

## Environment info
Operating System:
Windows 7, 64bit
Compiler:

Package used (Python/R/Scala/Julia):
C++, Visual Studio 2013/2015
MXNet version:
windows prebuild Yajiedesign release 20170902
Or if installed from source:
https://github.com/yajiedesign/mxnet/releases/tag/20170902
##

I'm running a simple CNN using C++ API. CPU only, no GPU. The program runs well without any batchnorm operation. Training and validation accuracy increased reasonably with increased epochs.

However, once the batchnorm was added, e.g. between the convolution and relu. Validation didn't work correctly. I can still see the training accuracy increased reasonably. However, validation accuracy didn't show any increasing trend even after long time training.

I tried on different computers. The same network runs correctly with pure Python for both networks with and without batchnorm operation. Tried GPU version with C++ also and encountering the same problem.

Any trick to use batchnorm with C++ package, esp. the CPU version?

Thanks for any help.

RowSparseNDArray.retain() is an infinite loop
issue_body: Spotted by @vandanavk and PyLint at https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/12200#issuecomment-413691405 [RowSparseNDArray.retain()](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/ndarray/sparse.py#L784-L790) would seem to recurse forever.

issue_title: Take up too much GPU memory
test_operator.test_unary_math_operators has fixed seed that can mask flakiness
issue_body:
The unit test in title have been using fixed seed to mask flakiness. Suggested action:
1. Evaluate whether the test is flaky without fixed seed. If not, remove seed. Else move to 2
2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

issue_title: gluon bug: AttributeError: '_thread._local' object has no attribute 'value'
issue_body:
## Description
Dear all, 

I am trying to run mxnet in a distributed HPC environment for embarrassingly parallel (distributed) runs.   
The goal is to use this for bayesian hyperparameter optimization, therefore all communication between nodes is nothing mxnet/gpu specific (lists of hyperparams, like learning rate, batch size etc). For my distributed needs I chose [ray](http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/). Each node has 4 gpus and runs a completely independent run from other nodes. However, I cannot even define a simple gluon layer within 
a ```ray.remote``` function. 

When I am using 2 (or more) nodes with this trivial example, everything is working: 

```Python
import os
import sys
import ray
import time

# mxnet gpu examples 
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import nd
import numpy as np


@ray.remote(num_gpus = 4)
def f():

    gpus = [int(x) for x in os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"].split(',')] # In case of multiple GPUs, comment out 2nd option. 
    tctx = [mx.gpu(i) for i in range(len(gpus))]
    a = nd.random.uniform(shape=[3,4,16,16],ctx=tctx[0])


    return a.asnumpy()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    ray.init( redis_address =  sys.argv[1])
    result1 = ray.get(f.remote())
    result2 = ray.get(f.remote())

    print (result1,result2)
```

However, when I try to use any gluon object that derives from HybridBlock, for example: 

```Python
@ray.remote(num_gpus=4)
def f(x):
    loss = gluon.loss.L2Loss()
    return x
```
I get an error. I've also tested ray with a simple pytorch nn (everything is working), so this is most probably a mxnet/gluon  problem.  

**edit**: The same problem and error message appears if I use [dask.distributed](http://distributed.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) for launching/managing the cluster.

issue_title: Numerical Gradient Improvement
issue_body:
@sxjscience: I noticed that the [numerical gradient checker](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/blob/2c8d209baac9ca262240dd3af59a14a5f49f8832/python/mxnet/test_utils.py#L260) uses `(f(x + h) - f(x)) / h` instead of the centered difference method `(f(x + h) - f(x -h)) / 2h`. The numerical error in the latter is of order `O(h^2)` and works much better in practice, whilst the former is `O(h)`. I've noticed that one needs to give a very large tolerance when doing grad checks in the test suite, perhaps because of this method.

Is there a reason for using the less precise method? Or can I change it?

argsort produces an ndarray which cannot be used for indexing an ndarray

ValueError: NDArray does not support slicing with key

Please also note that argsort returns a NDAarray of double and not of int as one would expect for indexes.

issue_title: Swagger Spec fails validation if response codes are not quoted
issue_body: using the yaml file from the swagger [editor](http://editor.swagger.io/#/) I saved it as a yaml file and tried to load it in the same way that the pyramid_swagger project loads the file. I get a "SwaggerValidationError: expected string or buffer" exception message. Debugging at that level shows that item passed down is an int not a string. Inspecting the dict that is generated from loading the file confirms that the response codes are rendered as integers. It is a rather nasty gotcha.

issue_title: 'also_return_response' should be added to CONFIG_DEFAULTS
issue_body:
This is currently breaking `make test` in bravado

```
E       KeyError: 'also_return_response'
```

This break is coming from https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/spec.py#L146, which is deleting this key because it's not whitelisted in CONFIG_DEFAULTS

Alternatively, we could just not delete extra keys

issue_title: Support for notifications through Amazon SNS
issue_body:
I've noticed that you guys support notifications through Amazon SES, raw SMTP, and Sendgrid. Would you also be open to a patch that added support for Amazon SNS?

It's the AWS-recommended way to send these types of automated notifications (SES is meant to be used more for marketing blasts). There's several benefits, including:
  * One-to-many subscriptions
  * Receiving SMS in addition to e-mail (with no extra work on the client)
  * Simpler setup (no need to mess with verifying your e-mail domain, etc)
  * Lower chance of getting caught in spam filters (since the e-mail comes from the trusted AWS domain rather than whatever random domain the user may have configured)

It shouldn't be hard to add, we're willing to send in a PR, I just want to make sure the team isn't philosophically opposed to it for some reason.

issue_title: Make resources pickleable/serializable

Would it be possible to have some way to serialize resources?

and the following test case:
```python
import botocore.session
import botocore.exceptions
import gc
import time
import sys


print(f"Using: {sys.argv[1]}")

def test(s3_client):
    try:
        method = getattr(s3_client, sys.argv[1])
        method(Bucket='archpi.dabase.com', Key='doesnotexist')
    except botocore.exceptions.ClientError as e:
        if e.response['ResponseMetadata']['HTTPStatusCode'] != 404:
            raise 

def do_get_obj(s3_client):
    response = s3_client.get_object(Bucket='archpi.dabase.com', Key='style.css')
    data = response["Body"].read()
    response["Body"].close()

def main():
    boto_session = botocore.session.get_session()
    boto_config = botocore.config.Config(connect_timeout=15, read_timeout=15, max_pool_connections=1)
    s3_client = boto_session.create_client('s3', config=boto_config)

    do_get_obj(s3_client)
    start = time.time()
    while True:
        test(s3_client)
        gc.collect()
        if time.time() - start > (60 * 5):
            break


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

and run with memory_profiler like so in python 3.6.5:

![boto_get_object](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4001789/40210900-66797b00-59fc-11e8-8e80-7beff3caae80.png)


NOTE: it seems critical do do the `get_object` before the `head_object` to trigger the leak

issue_title: Adding LFS tracked files to index
issue_body:
I'm writing a script to take snapshots from SVN URLs and add them to Git in a subfolder. Bringing LFS into the fold it seems like calling Repository.index.add_all isn't working quite right. The files that should be tracked seem to get put in to _.git/lfs/objects_ as expected, but `git lfs ls-files` doesn't list any files. I changed this line of my code

`repo.index.add_all([projectPath])`

to this

```python
        check_call(["/usr/bin/git","add","--all",projectPath])
        repo.index.read()
```

And that's fixed it, but I'd prefer to have the code self contained with library calls.

issue_title: Json format changed
issue_body:
I have been using the API successfully to integrate Categories and Products with existing legacy systems a couple of weeks ago. Developed everything and now it is time to test in the production environment. While in Dvmt environment each time I executed 

r = wcapi.get("products/categories?per_page=100")
vWooJason = json.loads(r.text)

the Json generated was like (reduced attributed for simplicity)
[
  { "id":788,
    "name":"Name1" },
  { "id":789,
    "name":"Name2" },
  { "id":790,
    "name":"NameN" }
]

therefore pagination and len(vWooJason) works fine.

Now without changing anything except installing WP-REST-API plugin on WordPress after executing

r = wcapi.get("products/categories?per_page=100")
vWooJason = json.loads(r.text)

the Json generated is

{  
   "product_categories":[  
      {  "id":788,
         "name":"Name1"  },
      {  "id":789,
         "name":"Name2"  },
      {  "id":790,
         "name":"NameN"  }
}

therefore pagination does not work in the same way and len(vWooJason) = 1 instad of the total number of categories.

Questions
  a) Any idea of why the format of the Json changed?
  b) Any idea on how to return to the original behaviour?

Regards,
Mariano

issue_title: [Windows] Unable to add files to index if corresponding object file is read-only
issue_body:
Seen on 
`OS`: Windows 7
`git.__version__`: 1.0.1
`python --version`: Python 3.4.3

#### Description
If an object file becomes read-only, trying to add the corresponding working directory file to an index causes a File Access Error:

Running the program below following with the object file for 'test.txt' set to read-only triggers the error (see Traceback at bottom).
This appears to be because the store() function of the underlying loose DB tries to remove the file before renaming a temp file onto it. However, on windows, read-only files cannot be removed with os.remove - one must remove the read only attribute first. 

Although this might seem like a farfetched scenario, in reality I've hit this in several real usage scenarios (though I have not been able to characterize exactly why an object file becomes read-only). So, I think this is generally valid (and indeed this would work fine on *nix due to the fact that read-only files can still be deleted with os.remove)

I do not know enough about the way GIT stores objects to say what the best solution would be here, but one option would be to check for this case and explicilty remove the read-only attribute before attempting to remove.

#### Program

    import git
    repo_path = r"C:\Users\ach\Documents\code\odds_ends\gptest"
    broken_path = r"C:\Users\ach\Documents\code\odds_ends\gptest\test.txt"
    repo = git.Repo(repo_path)
    idx = repo.index
    idx.add(items=[broken_path])

#### Traceback

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "C:/Users/ach/Documents/code/odds_ends/gitpy_broken.py", line 7, in <module>
        idx.add(items=[broken_path])
      File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\git\index\base.py", line 726, in add

      File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\git\util.py", line 54, in wrapper

      File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\git\index\util.py", line 84, in set_git_working_dir
        return func(self, *args, **kwargs)
      File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\git\index\base.py", line 612, in _entries_for_paths
        entries_added.append(self._store_path(filepath, fprogress))
      File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\git\index\base.py", line 580, in _store_path
        istream = self.repo.odb.store(IStream(Blob.type, st.st_size, stream))
      File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\gitdb\db\git.py", line 73, in store

issue_title: Development section of README should be more explicit about use of git clone
issue_body:
It currently says to `git@github.com:dropbox/nsot.git`, which will fail if someone is not familiar w/ GitHub or has not setup an SSH key on their account, or their host does not have their public key.

There should be a callout to setup SSH keys w/ GitHub, or just have the docs point to the `https` URL instead to avoid this potential issue.

issue_title: Inconsistent sys_platform markers when running `pipenv install`
documentation website is down
Should replace date_range with rrule?
condenseFlow in combination with flowLevel 0 is broken
issue_body:
Simple sample code:

```
var jsYaml = require("js-yaml")
console.log(jsYaml.safeDump({ a: "a", b: "b", c: "c" }, { flowLevel: 0, condenseFlow: true }))
```

This results in a broken string like this:
```
{"a":a", b":b", c":c}\n
```

issue_title: Force js-yaml to 1.1 mode
issue_body: I'm upgrading from js-yaml v3.2.7 to x3.7.0, but need yaml to be parsed to 1.1 spec. My understanding is that docker compose supports yaml 1.1. Is there a way to force js-yaml to parse to yaml 1.1?

issue_title: Rename `px`, `py` variables in `draw.bezier_curve`
issue_body:
## Description
See https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/draw/_draw.pyx#L776.

```
    px.extend(rr)
    py.extend(cc)
```
Should be renamed (`px->py`, `py->px`) to avoid confusion.

issue_title: RFC Problems during module separation
issue_body:
We started to rip out certain modules from Invenio core to improve maintainability and to speed up development. This issue contains some of the problems, that I faced during that process:

## Autodiscovery
**Problem:** Right now, modules have to be specified using the `PACKAGES` config variable. That makes creating overlays more difficult and error-prone, because devs need to specify the desired set twice (once in `setup.py`, a second time in `config.py`).

**Solution:** Implement entry points.

**Status:** Planned. 

## Test Overlays
**Problem:** To test a certain module, other modules that use that functionality are needed. This is especially true for visual test and automated browser tests, but also holds for presenting a good demo use case for a module.

**Solution:** Add a fake package to the modules to build a demo overlay.

**Status:** Implemented in https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-jsonschemas/pull/1 and https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/3380.

##  "Module" vs "Package"
**Problem:** We somehow mix up the terms `module` and `package`. Our [cookiecutter template](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module) clearly calls the ripped out *things* modules and the Python parts packages. The dynamic loading system in `config.py` calls the single parts packages. In theory, a module can implement multiple packages, but I've never seen this in the wild and I think we do not really encourage people to do so.

**Solution:** Write some docs to clarify the module system, including the installation and loading as well as the relations `overlay->modules->packages`.

**Status:** TODO.

## require.js
**Problem:** Require.js uses [`settings.js`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/invenio/base/static/js/settings.js) and [`build.js`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/invenio/base/static/js/build.js) for getting the relations between the our JS files and vendors files correct. We've already seen that this [is not working well for overlays](https://github.com/zenodo/zenodo/blob/master/zenodo/base/static/js/settings.js#L164).

**Solution:** Replace the static version of the mentioned files with a dynamic version and some kind of registry.

**Status:** TODO.

## Upgrade Recipes
**Problem:** Right now, upgrades have to mention dependencies explicitly. Now imagine the scenario that we've faced during the [InnoDB upgrade](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/3355). There we needed to fix module bugs before doing another global upgrade. Stating the fact that the core should not know about available modules, this upgrade would never be possible and [already let to some issues](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/issues/3406).

**Solution:** Implement the ability to specify a `before` relation of upgrades as well (in addition to the `after` relation).

**Status:** TODO.

## Load Order
**Problem:** Module devs currently have no way to control the load order of there module and having [cyclic dependencies](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/issues/3120#issuecomment-103816619) does not make this easier.

**Solution:** Using *Lazy{String,Dict,...}` and hope that it works? Leaving it to the overlay/site admin (i.e. `config.py`)?

**Status:** TODO.

----------------------------

**to be continued**

**Problem:**
The way files are currently created would leave files on the filesystem in case of rollback/crash without any reference in the database.

**Planned solution:**

- [ ] modify files creation in buckets so that the worst case scenario is a dangling file row in the database referencing no file on the filesystem.

issue_title: Sync namespace packages approach
issue_body:
Hello.

As mentioned in Python docs [0]:
> **Every** distribution that uses the namespace package must include an identical `__init__.py`. If any distribution does not, it will cause the namespace logic to fail and the other sub-packages will not be importable. Any additional code in `__init__.py` will be inaccessible.

but it seems that zope.deprecation [1] and zope.configuration [2] have different content in namespace `__init__.py` files than zope.interface.

It would be nice to sync namespace `__init__.py` files across modules maintained by Zope foundation so they will be installable in parallel.

[0] https://packaging.python.org/guides/packaging-namespace-packages/#pkg-resources-style-namespace-packages
[1] https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.deprecation/blob/master/src/zope/__init__.py
[2] https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.configuration/blob/master/src/zope/__init__.py

Possibly related to:
* https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.interface/issues/107
* https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.interface/issues/68
* https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.interface/issues/97

issue_title: Built in EventDispatch silently fails
issue_body:
The default `EventDispatcher` only handles network related errors and ignores HTTP errors(`500`, `503`, etc) without any logging messages

```
try:
  if event.http_verb == enums.HTTPVerbs.GET:
    requests.get(event.url, params=event.params, timeout=10)
  elif event.http_verb == enums.HTTPVerbs.POST:
    requests.post(event.url, json=event.params, headers=event.headers, timeout=10)
except request_exception.RequestException as error:
  logging.error('Dispatch event failed. Error: %s' % str(error))
```

HTTP errors in `requests` do not raise Exceptions
```
In [2]: requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/500')
Out[2]: <Response [500]>   # This is an object, not an raise error therefore not caught by try/except
```

You would need to raise from the response
```
response = requests.get(event.url, params=event.params, timeout=10)
In [4]: response.raise_for_status()
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
HTTPError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-4-46d4bf99ee16> in <module>()
----> 1 requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/500').raise_for_status()

python3.6/site-packages/requests/models.py in raise_for_status(self)
    937 
    938         if http_error_msg:
--> 939             raise HTTPError(http_error_msg, response=self)
    940 
    941     def close(self):

HTTPError: 500 Server Error: INTERNAL SERVER ERROR for url: https://httpbin.org/status/500
```

issue_title: Would it be possbile to include the .dlls in the binary download?
issue_body:
Hi,

would it be possible to include the .dll files in the binary downloads for Windows?
The .dlls are kind of needed to make the JNI wrappers work. This is especially a problem for the encoder as there is no Java implementation (which is a huge adoption problem IMHO).

I already tried to build the .dlls myself with CMake and MinGW, but I was not able to make the JNI encoder wrapper work with them. I always got an UnsatisfiedLinkError on instantiation with libbrotlicommon.dll and libbrotlienc.dll loaded.

>java.lang.UnsatisfiedLinkError: org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI.nativeCreate([J)Ljava/nio/ByteBuffer;
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI.nativeCreate(Native Method)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI.access$000(EncoderJNI.java:15)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.EncoderJNI$Wrapper.<init>(EncoderJNI.java:36)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.Encoder.compress(Encoder.java:159)
        at org.brotli.wrapper.enc.Encoder.compress(Encoder.java:196)

I suspect it has to do with [gcc using symbols incompatible with JNI](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/10274278/jni-unsatisfiedlinkerror-on-native-method) and me not able to make CMake to do the right thing.

Anyways, it would be nice if we could have the Windows libs along with the executable similar to the other platforms.

issue_title: Please publish Sparkling Water POM files with Spark project dependencies in the "provided", not "compile", scope
issue_body: The Spark dependencies for Sparkling Water as of this writing are defined in the "compile" scope. For example, see http://search.maven.org/remotecontent?filepath=ai/h2o/sparkling-water-core_2.11/2.2.8/sparkling-water-core_2.11-2.2.8.pom. Spark apps run in a container that provides the API and implementation at runtime. Declaring Spark as a "compile" scope dependency can lead to the introduction of conflicting or duplicate classfiles at runtime. This is especially true when building "assembly" jars or "fat jars" which include compile dependencies for deployment, however this is also a problem when using a classloader that can load maven library dependencies at runtime (e.g. Ammonite). See https://maven.apache.org/guides/introduction/introduction-to-dependency-mechanism.html#Dependency_Scope and http://ammonite.io/#import$ivy.

issue_body:
In order to avoid complex handling of the results of the feature functions I would like to suggest that we adopt a "duck" format for the output of the latter even for functions such as "get_n_segments".

For example, if all the outputs were numpy arrays (bonus if the are flattened as well) then a special handling would not be necessary and the stat extraction would apply the same for any feature function.

Do you guys think that this would cause any kind of conflict?

issue_title: FontAwesome 5 causes high CPU utilization in browser
issue_body:
I'm seeing high CPU utilization in Firefox, Chrome, & Safari with FontAwesome 5.

The examples below were captured when a single page was sitting idle. No user activity was taking place, nor was other JavaScript being executed.  

## Firefox

The Firefox process **FirefoxCP Web Content** typically consumes between 5%-10% CPU when not using FontAwesome 5.

After FontAwesome 5 is added, the **FirefoxCP Web Content** process consistently consumes between 40%-60%.

### Firefox without FontAwesome 5

![Firefox - FontAwesome 5](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32920/35974323-dd4c5482-0c95-11e8-8b5a-f0fc96ed9e75.png)

### Firefox with FontAwesome 5

![Firefox + FontAwesome 5](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32920/35974271-9df3fe98-0c95-11e8-8ad2-ce4e40cc36d2.png)

## Safari

Safari typically consumes between 0%-5% CPU when not using FontAwesome 5.

After FontAwesome 5 is added, Safari consistently consumes between 20%-40% CPU.

### Safari without FontAwesome 5

![Safari - FontAwesome 5](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32920/35974558-cb0ef76a-0c96-11e8-9f4c-1deb74239be8.png)

### Safari with FontAwesome 5

![Safari + FontAwesome 5](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32920/35974628-12dfb4e4-0c97-11e8-8384-49f2b18060aa.png)

issue_title: Add ast36
issue_body:
I've just tried `mypy --fast-parser --python-version 3.6` on code that utilizes some new Python 3.6 features and it failed.

Breaking features:
* formatted string literals, [PEP 498](https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.6.html#whatsnew36-pep498) -- `error: invalid syntax`
* asynchronous generators, [PEP 525](https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.6.html#whatsnew36-pep525) -- `error: 'yield' in async function`
* asynchronous comprehensions, [PEP 530](https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.6.html#whatsnew36-pep530) -- `error: invalid syntax`

issue_title: Cannot show details of user story if parent is a saga feature.
issue_body:
I have a user story whose parent is a Saga Feature.  When I attempt to use details() on this I trigger the following traceback:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "UserStoryQuery.py", line 18, in <module>
    print(story.details())
  File "C:\Python35\lib\site-packages\pyral\entity.py", line 383, in details
    anv = "%s (OID  %s  FomattedID  %s)" % (anv, value.oid, value.FormattedID)
  File "C:\Python35\lib\site-packages\pyral\entity.py", line 147, in __getattr__
    item = response.content[rallyEntityTypeName]
KeyError: 'Sagafeature'
```

It looks like some parsing is being done of the lines in this area which is forcing PortfolioItem_SagaFeature to Sagafeature.  If I add a simple `.replace('Sagafeature', SagaFeature')` to line 114 like so:
`rallyEntityTypeName = entity_path.split('/')[-1].capitalize().replace('Sagafeature','SagaFeature')`

This seems to restore operation.  I'm sure there's a more elegant solution though.

issue_title: Support for catkin_install_python
issue_body:
Per http://docs.ros.org/kinetic/api/catkin/html/howto/format2/installing_python.html, one should use `catkin_install_python` to install python scripts. However, catkin_lint does not recognize this command and consequently warns about uninstalled files.

Example CMakeLists.txt:

```
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 2.8.3)
project(my_test_project)

find_package(catkin REQUIRED)

catkin_package(
	CATKIN_DEPENDS
	DEPENDS
)

install(
	DIRECTORY launch
	DESTINATION
		${CATKIN_PACKAGE_SHARE_DESTINATION}
)

catkin_install_python(PROGRAMS
	scripts/${PROJECT_NAME}
	DESTINATION ${CATKIN_PACKAGE_SHARE_DESTINATION}/scripts
)
```

where `catkin lint -W2` complains about `my_test_project: warning: executable file 'scripts/my_test_project' is not installed` even though it is.

```
In [1]: import dynet as dy

issue_title: Giant loss for LogSoftmax when Auto-Batching on GPU
issue_body:
Hi,

thank you for the awesome work!

It seems to me that autobatching (on GPU) doesn't work as expected when there is a sum within a logsoftmax.

Here is an MWE (created by simplifying the code where I originally ran into the problem):

```
import dynet as dy
import random

def run():
  b_out = dy.vecInput(46349)
  b_out.set([0] * 46349)
  losses = []
  length = random.randint(5,50)
  for i in range(0, length):
      log_probs = dy.log_softmax(b_out + b_out)
      losses.append(dy.pick(log_probs, random.randint(0,1000)))
  return dy.esum(losses)
           
model = dy.Model()
while True:
  dy.renew_cg()
  losses = []
  for data in range(0,64): 
    losses.append(run())
  total_loss = dy.esum(losses).scalar_value()
  print('loss per sentence: {}'.format(total_loss / 64))
```
When I originally ran into the problem, the sum inside the softmax was an MLP (which is why I need this construct), but it turned out that summing constants was enough to create the problem.

Saving this in session.py and calling
`python session.py --dynet-mem 5000 --dynet-autobatch 1 --dynet-gpu`
I get impossibly huge losses like 1e13 for many (but not all) iterations. Removing either `--dynet-autobatch 1` or `--dynet-gpu`, the loss is about 300, which is expected. When removing `--dynet-mem 5000`, the bad losses turn to NaN.

The problem disappears when changing 
`      log_probs = dy.log_softmax(b_out + b_out)`
to
`      log_probs = dy.log_softmax(b_out)`
b_out is zero, and its dimension is within the usual range in language modeling. Therefore, it is hard for me to imagine how this could be due to numerical problems.

Is there a way of implementing this without running into this difficulty? Or is this a problem inside the autobatching mechanism?

Thank you for any help!

issue_title: Access to foreign keys does not result in a filter on the tenant_id
issue_body:
The following:
```python
from django.db import models
from django_multitenant import set_current_tenant, TenantModel

class Tenant(models.Model)
	pass


class Bar(TenantModel):
    tenant = models.ForeignKey(Tenant)
	tenant_id = 'tenant'

	
class Foo(TenantModel):
	tenant = models.ForeignKey(Tenant)
	tenant_id = 'tenant'

	bar = models.ForeignKey(Bar)
	
	

tenant = Tenant.objects.create()
bar = Bar.objects.create(tenant=tenant)
foo = Foo.objects.create(tenant=tenant, bar=bar)

set_current_tenant(tenant)

print(Foo.objects.get().bar)
```
...will output this SQL:
```sql
SELECT "foo"."id", "foo"."tenant_id", "foo"."bar_id" FROM "foo" WHERE "foo"."tenant_id" = '3d340b31-cd78-4224-9ac0-00455933c1b8'::uuid
SELECT "bar"."id", "bar"."tenant_id" FROM "bar" WHERE "bar"."id" = 2
```

Changing TenantModel to include:

```
class TenantModel:
  class Meta:
    base_manager_name = 'tenantManager'
  tenantManager = TenantManager()
```

seems to fix the issue

issue_title: core.Iterator, max_results and page size
issue_body:
__Background__

Google RPCs that enumerate a collection page by page support a request field we'll call "page size," which is the maximum number of items to return in the call. (Confusingly, the older HTTP-based APIs call this query parameter `maxResults`, a fact you're better off forgetting for the remainder of this issue.)

The `google.api.core.page_iterator.Iterator` class abstracts over paging RPCs, allowing item-by-item iteration while performing RPCs as needed. For users of `Iterator`, page size is an implementation detail; it only affects performance.

`Iterator` can be initialized with `max_results`, which is the maximum number of results to return. This is mostly a convenience feature: one could always just stop iterating after the desired number of items have been retrieved. There is also a small performance benefit available, in that the iterator could adjust the page size so that no more than `max_results` values are ever returned. (The `HTTPIterator` subclass of `Iterator` does in fact do this; the other subclasses do not set the page size.)

__Question__

How should methods that use `Iterator` handle page size?

Possibilities:

1. They should not support it. This is the current situation, at least in the `bigquery` module.

2. The `max_results` parameter that these methods accept should be re-interpreted as page size, instead of meaning the maximum number of results for the entire iteration.

3. Both `max_results`, with its current meaning, and `page_size` should be supported. (This would entail modifications to `Iterator`.)

/cc @tswast @lukesneeringer @dhermes @jonparrott @tseaver
